1.Title: Response Time Improves, Then Spikes
1.Insight: For the endpoint Homepage, a dramatic decrease in response time alongside an increase in RPS suggests initial server inefficiency followed by optimization or stabilization, but a spike later indicates a temporary bottleneck.
1.Details: [Response time](#ClientMetrics) started at **3430ms** at 9:24 PM UTC, dropped to 1890ms at 9:25 PM UTC, and stabilized between 1840ms–1850ms from 9:26 PM UTC–9:26 PM UTC. A spike to **2840ms** occurred at 9:25 PM UTC. [Requests per second](#ClientMetrics) rose steadily from 3.0 to **6.2 RPS (at t10)**, then dipped to 3.0 RPS at 9:26 PM UTC, suggesting that the server initially struggled with the load but adapted before facing a brief performance bottleneck.
1.Recommendation: Consider reviewing server auto-scaling settings and application bottlenecks causing temporary response time spikes. Analyze logs around 9:25 PM UTC for possible resource contention or transient issues affecting latency.

2.Summary: Load test (2 min, 44 sec, peak 652 VUs) completed with minor latency spikes, passing all test criteria. Client-side response times peaked at 3,430 ms initially before stabilizing; throughput fluctuated but no errors occurred.

1.Title: Response Time Spike Correlates With RPS Increase
1.Insight: Response time for Homepage peaked at **2840ms** as requests per second also reached its highest value (**6.2 RPS**). This suggests the server may not handle increased load efficiently.
1.Details: [Response time](#ClientMetrics) for Homepage spiked from 1890ms at 9:25 PM UTC to **2840ms at 9:25 PM UTC**, then decreased to 1840ms by 9:26 PM UTC. [Requests per second](#ClientMetrics) climbed from 3.0 RPS at 9:24 PM UTC to a peak of **6.2 RPS at 9:25 PM UTC**, then declined back to 3.0 RPS at 9:26 PM UTC.
1.Recommendation: Consider optimizing server-side resources or backend processing to handle increased load more efficiently. You may also review server scaling and caching strategies to prevent latency spikes under high RPS.

2.Title: Stabilization After Initial Latency Drop
2.Insight: Client response time for Homepage dropped significantly from **3430ms** to under **1900ms**, coinciding with moderate increases in RPS. This reflects improved performance, possibly as traffic transitioned or warmed-up.
2.Details: [Response time](#ClientMetrics) decreased from **3430ms at 9:24 PM UTC** to 2080ms by 9:25 PM UTC, and further down to 1890ms at 9:25 PM UTC. [Requests per second](#ClientMetrics) increased steadily from 3.0 RPS at 9:24 PM UTC to 6.2 RPS at 9:25 PM UTC.
2.Recommendation: Consider further tuning for initial request processing or warming up the application before peak load, especially if cold-starts or initial resource allocation is a bottleneck.

3.Title: Periodic Latency Volatility During Load
3.Insight: During sustained traffic, response time for Homepage exhibited volatility, rising to **2310ms** before stabilizing below **1900ms**. This aligns with fluctuating RPS and indicates transient server bottlenecking.
3.Details: [Response time](#ClientMetrics) fluctuated between 9:25 PM UTC and 9:26 PM UTC, rising to **2310ms at 9:26 PM UTC** before stabilizing below 1900ms at 9:26 PM UTC and 9:26 PM UTC. [Requests per second](#ClientMetrics) also showed minor fluctuations in the same interval.
3.Recommendation: Monitor backend resource utilization and review auto-scaling configuration. If possible, increase server scaling responsiveness or optimize middleware for smoother traffic handling.

4.Summary: Load test (2 min, 40 sec, 652 VUs) completed with minor latency spikes but met all defined criteria. Client-side response times briefly peaked above 3s before stabilizing, with throughput showing fluctuations; root cause unclear due to missing server metrics.
